data.title: ‘Cash-in-hand barbers are an attack on businesses like ours’
data.description:
With just over two weeks to go until the most anticipated Budget in recent memory, workers and businesses of all stripes are clamouring for some reprieve from Rachel Reeves.

The Chancellor hammered employers last year with a rise in National Insurance contributions, dealing a £23bn blow to the economy, and adding 2pc to employers’ payroll costs.

But Britain’s hairdressers argue they face an existential threat posed by the spread of cash-in-hand barbershops, made worse by a tax burden that is three times the size of a typical high street business.

“We have 16 barbershops in our area now, and I don’t know how they’ve managed to pay the high rent for the high street premises,” says James Talbot, who has run barbershops in Oxfordshire for 13 years.

Many barbershops operate in ways designed to minimise tax, including by disguising employment by classifying workers as self-employed when they are effectively employees. This set-up allows business to limit their profits to stay under the VAT threshold and dodge higher tax bills.

A report by the British Hair Consortium and CBI Economics found that a so-called “disguised employment business” contributed less than half the tax of a traditional employer business.

Local Data Company figures show 18,642 barbershops were operating in the UK as of 2024, with hundreds more opening each year despite the relative decline of the high street in big cities and market towns.

Self-employment in the hairdressing and beauty industry rose from 46pc in 2006 to 63pc in 2024. By 2028, nine in 10 hairdressers will be self-employed, CBI Economics said.

Traditional hairdressers point to a growing hidden economy where substantial income is going untaxed, giving an unfair edge to cash-in-hand businesses, which can charge lower prices.

Richard Tice, deputy leader of Reform UK, vowed last year to crack down on barbershops, dubbing the cash-in-hand businesses “another great British taxpayer rip-off”.

Earlier this year HMRC and the National Crime Agency’s “Operation Machinize” raided more than 250 barbershops, vape shops and nail bars across England and Wales suspected of underpaying tax.

The raids also suggested some barbershops served as fronts for criminal activity, including the sale of illicit vapes.

Those prosecuted included Omead Ibrahim Abdullah Khoshnaw, who traded as Jimmy’s Barbers in Sunderland.

Khoshnaw was fined £35,100 after HMRC ruled he had defaulted on £50,100 of tax between 2016 and 2023. The business is no longer trading.

“My understanding if you’re taking cash only, that’s an opportunity not to declare turnover. We have a card machine – all our turnover gets recognised,” says Talbot. “It’s unfair on us and our staff – and it’s unfair on society.

“We want to encourage entrepreneurs to contribute admirably to our failing society. We have no money in the system because lots of businesses are consciously keeping themselves below the £90,000 threshold.”

In addition to competitors that can undercut their prices, hairdressers face a high tax burden compared to a typical high street business.

In an example given by the CBI, a salon with an annual turnover of £1.2m would pay £80,000 more in VAT and £480,000 more in labour costs than an equivalent high street retail business.

Salons’ tax bills are so high because around 60pc of their turnover goes to paying staff, compared to about 12pc for a typical high street business.

Selling haircuts means almost every pound a salon earns is taxed more – first by labour taxes, such as NI and pensions, then by VAT.

However, while retail and hospitality businesses mostly pay VAT, they can claim much of it back because they buy goods, so they’re taxed less on business inputs.

“The playing field is already vastly unlevel,” says Toby Dicker, of the Hair Consortium. “Roughly 60pc of our costs go on labour – five times the amount of other businesses. We’re the hardest hit on the high street.”

Industry bodies are calling on Ms Reeves to lower the VAT threshold for salons to “level the playing field” with other businesses. Dicker says Labour is sympathetic to the idea, even as the Treasury mulls a manifesto-breaching rise in income tax to balance the books.

The CBI Economics report also warned that the sector’s capacity to offer training for young workers was in critical decline. The report estimated that by 2027, the number of apprenticeship starts was projected to reach zero, costing £3.2bn in productivity benefits.

“We’re unable to take on the youngsters we want to take on,” says Talbot of his Oxfordshire salons. “We want to see our trade evolve, but it’s losing its trendy and fashionable edge if the area is filled with barbershops.”

The Treasury was approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Asda market share hits record low as supermarket’s sales slide
data.description:
Asda’s share of the grocery market has plunged to a new record low, piling pressure on boss Allan Leighton to show signs of a turnaround after a year in charge.

The supermarket’s sales slump deepened in the 12-week period to Nov 2 in what was the 20th consecutive month of decline, according to Worldpanel figures.

Sales tumbled by 3.9pc, compared to a 3.2pc drop in the prior period. Asda’s share of the grocery market slipped to 11.8pc from 12.7pc a year ago when Mr Leighton took charge.

It means Asda’s market share is at the lowest level since Worldpanel started collecting data in 2011. Its market share was 14.8pc when the supermarket was taken over by private equity firm TDR Capital and brothers Mohsin and Zuber Issa in 2021.

The decline is a major blow to Mr Leighton, who has promised to revive the struggling grocer after taking over as chief executive last November.

Mr Leighton, who previously ran Asda in the late 1990s, had said his revival drive would take at least three years, likening it to “climbing Everest”.

Since the start of the year, Mr Leighton has pushed through a series of price cuts and spent more on improving the availability of products in stores.

However, the latest figures raise questions over whether he is doing enough to put Asda back on the right track.

Clive Black, an analyst at Shore Capital, says he is surprised that “Asda’s trading is not in a much more stable place, which suggests considerable brand damage in recent years”.

In particular, Asda’s major price-cutting drive – which kicked off at the start of the year with the relaunch of its “Rollback” programme – would have been expected to start winning customers back.

Mr Black says it is not clear whether Asda “underestimated the competitor response”.

“If Asda does not show greater signs of trading stability soon, notably after the anniversary of the introduction of RollBack, then there will be elevated grounds for concern,” he said.

It is not just that Asda is struggling to stem falling sales; customers are also taking a dimmer view of the supermarket.

In Sainsbury’s interim results last week, the grocer highlighted recent survey figures from YouGov, which it claimed showed that Asda ranked well behind competitors on customers’ price perception.

For Mr Leighton, it thrusts a fresh spotlight on what he is doing to improve performance.

Much of the recent focus has involved getting staff on board with his strategy, with the executive taking an unconventional approach to win over employees.

Last month, Asda hosted a series of team-building days at its head office for which it built a faux “base camp” of Everest, including erecting a rock-climbing structure in its car park for staff to climb.

A spokesman for Asda said: “At the start of the year, we set out a strategy to re-establish Asda as the UK’s cheapest traditional supermarket, improve product availability and enhance the customer experience.

“We’ve made solid progress across all areas, particularly on price, where we are now significantly cheaper than competitors’ base and loyalty prices, despite rising food price inflation. We recognise it will take time to rebuild market share, but we remain confident in the strength of our customer proposition going forward.”

data.title: ChatGPT founder backs baby gene-editing business with husband
data.description:
The founder of ChatGPT is backing a controversial genetic engineering startup that aims to eliminate hereditary diseases from babies.

Sam Altman, the chief executive of ChatGPT owner OpenAI, is among investors in a $30m (£22.7m) funding round for Preventive, a US business aiming to “correct devastating genetic conditions for future children”.

The company, headquartered in San Francisco, has raised funds from a clutch of technology billionaires and is reportedly seeking a jurisdiction to legally conduct its experiments, with the United Arab Emirates under consideration.

Mr Altman has backed Preventive along with his spouse, Oliver Mulherin. They welcomed a baby earlier this year via a surrogate.

More than 70 countries, including the US and the UK, ban making heritable edits to the human genome that can be passed down to children.

While genetic editing is widespread in agriculture, for instance, to reduce crop diseases, there have been longstanding ethical and safety concerns about testing the technology on human embryos.

There are fears that tampering with human DNA could lead to unforeseen mutations or lead to a rise in eugenics and “designer babies”.

Other investors include Brian Armstrong, chief executive of cryptocurrency exchange Coinbase.

Mr Armstrong said: “Research should be done to determine if safe and effective therapies can be developed to cure these diseases at birth.”

‘Potential to save millions’

While Preventive admits gene editing is a “controversial field”, it says the technology has the “potential to save millions of lives”.

“Our mission is to determine whether the newest generation of gene editing technologies can be used safely and responsibly to correct devastating genetic conditions for future children,” it said.

“If proven to be safe, we believe preventive gene editing could be one of the most important health technologies of the century.”

Despite the concerns about testing on human embryos, US doctors earlier this year used similar gene-editing techniques to treat a baby soon after birth who had a rare and life-threatening genetic condition.

The baby, known as DJ, had been born without a key liver enzyme that converts ammonia into urea, which risked causing extreme damage to his internal organs, including his brain.

Researchers have now asked US regulators to approve this kind of bespoke therapy for more patients with genetic diseases.

In 2018, a Chinese scientist conducted the first birth of human twins using genetically modified embryos to protect against HIV. He was jailed for 18 months for the rogue experiment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Forced out of retirement by a £25k bill: MPs probe freeholder over service charges
data.description:
Leaseholders from across Britain have been called to give evidence to MPs as part of a campaign to hold managing agents to account for alleged failings.

Twelve residents last week spoke on a range of alleged issues experienced under the management of FirstPort, the UK’s largest leasehold management company. These include escalating charges, poor service quality, opaque fees and a lack of communication.

Rachel Blake, Labour MP for Cities of London and Westminster, said she had “heard enough excuses” from FirstPort, after Martin King, the company’s managing director, pledged to make improvements to operations at a meeting with MPs in January.

“FirstPort must now demonstrate proper accountability,” she said. “Leaseholders deserve transparency and timely repairs.”

Currently, England and Wales’s five million leaseholders are required to pay a service charge to cover the upkeep of shared areas in their building and grounds – at an average of £2,300 per year.

French-owned FirstPort manages around 300,000 properties across Britain, with more than one in 60 under its stewardship.

But in the past year, it has fallen under growing scrutiny as part of a wider review of the UK’s service charge system – described by Labour MP Justin Madders as the “PPI of the housebuilding industry” for the scale of alleged misconduct.

Facebook group Firstport Resident’s Action Group has 13,500 members and receives dozens of claims every week about issues such as delays to urgent repairs and missing reserve funds.

‘Horrendous since day one’

Chia Yen Oh, who attended the parliamentary inquiry session on behalf of a housing estate in Colchester, Essex, claimed she had been left feeling anxious and stressed by her attempts to challenge FirstPort’s raising of charges and alleged poor service provision.

“It has been horrendous since day one,” she said. “It’s just always niggling. I’m a single mum, I’ve got two part-time jobs and a business and I have to do this on top of it. It’s very time-consuming and draining.”

Sarah Colvin, whose housing estate in Nottingham was represented by Louise Scull at the inquiry, said her husband, Ray, had been forced out of retirement after FirstPort neglect left them facing a £25,000 bill for repairs on their Grade II listed block of apartments in Edwalton Hall.

After their freeholder got rid of FirstPort following complaints about a lack of maintenance, the new managing agent said it would cost around £250,000 between 10 flats to restore the block. This was in spite of paying thousands every year to FirstPort for the upkeep of the building and grounds, and continuing to pay around £4,000 a year in fees.

“It’s going to knock our life savings out of kilter – £25,000 upfront is a great deal of money to find. We have five grandchildren and we were hoping we’d be able to help ease their way at university.

“My husband is 75 and he’s back to working as hard as he ever did.”

Ms Colvin said a separate issue involving a three-month delay fixing a lift in the building also left an elderly resident “effectively housebound”.

‘Power imbalance’

In a House of Commons debate on the topic last Thursday, Conservative MP Rebecca Paul said issues concerning FirstPort at a housing estate in her Surrey constituency were “symptomatic of an industry that prioritises extracting maximum value from leaseholders regardless of the human cost”.

“They are opportunists making money from an inequitable system,” she said. “This fundamental power imbalance must be addressed. It must be easier for leaseholders to take their business elsewhere.”

Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister, said he would request a meeting with Mr King to address concerns directly.

In 2024, average service charges rose by 11pc, well above the inflation rate of 2.5pc.

A rise in complaints about soaring fees and poor quality work has prompted cross-party MPs to summon managing agents, including FirstPort, RMG, and Trinity to Parliament for questioning.

The Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act passed in the final days of the Conservative government aimed to address some of the common issues concerning service charges.

These include giving leaseholders more transparency about what their fees are being spent on, as well as making it easier to challenge any increases.

But MPs have called for more radical reforms that would see local authorities “adopt” communal lands and infrastructure on the completion of new estates, thus taking it out of the hands of management companies.

MP Fleur Anderson criticised the Leasehold Act, calling it “rushed and poorly drafted” and said it failed to ban new leasehold homes.

James Naish, MP for Rushcliffe, the constituency in which Ms Colvin and her husband live, said the Government needed to tackle issues around leasehold management companies before it embarked on a building blitz.

“We’ve got to get the long-term reform right,” he said. “If we’re going to be building a million or more homes, there needs to be the right legislative framework in place, instead of creating more of these problems.”

Dozens of MPs have written to FirstPort after listening to complaints from constituents, outlining specific complaints and making fresh demands to improve their service with a timeline for tangible improvement.

A spokesman for FirstPort said: “We conduct regular inspections across all the communal areas we manage and work closely with specialist contractors to deliver tailored maintenance programmes. However, issues can occasionally arise, particularly in older buildings.

“As soon as we became aware of the water ingress at Edwalton Hall, we began investigating the repair works needed. Responsibility for completing these works has since been handed over to the new managing agent. While we have not seen the full scope of the planned repairs, our experience shows that costs associated with Grade II listed buildings can be considerably higher.

“As a business, we have made significant improvements to our systems, ways of working and customer service, and we are also continuing to invest in our people, ensuring they have the right skills and qualifications.”

data.title: British Airways owner threatens to shift investment to Madrid amid Heathrow row
data.description:
The chief executive of British Airways (BA) has warned that its Spanish owner could ditch investment in Heathrow if the bill for the airport’s third runway is too high.

Sean Doyle said International Airlines Group (IAG) is concerned by the cost of Heathrow’s £49bn expansion, which will be largely paid for by carriers and passengers.

He said IAG does not support plans to move the M25 to accommodate the expansion, while he also urged officials to consider pursuing a shorter, cheaper runway.

A failure to keep passenger fees under control risks making Heathrow less attractive, Mr Doyle said, while it could also lead to IAG prioritising investment in its airlines overseas, including Spain’s Iberia and Ireland’s Aer Lingus.

He said: “We’re part of a group and the group allocates capital on the basis of performance.

“If Heathrow becomes uncompetitive and that impacts the performance of an airline like BA, there’ll be opportunities to put that capital elsewhere.”

Mr Doyle added: “Madrid is flying at the minute, with Iberia reporting very strong results, and Barcelona is an expanding market we have a very strong presence in. Dublin has built a new runway for a couple of billion, so there’s headroom there.

“There will be debates about competitiveness and airport charges when it comes to the way IAG allocates capital. We’re not at that point yet but that’s why, for everybody’s sake, a competitive Heathrow is very important.”

British Airways stepped up its campaign against escalating costs at Heathrow as the Government prepares to choose between competing runway plans by the end of the month.

Heathrow Airport’s own proposals call for the construction of a 3.5km runway built over the M25, lifting annual passenger numbers from 82 million to 150 million.

A rival plan from Surinder Arora, the hotel tycoon, envisages a 2,800m strip that would be unable to take the very biggest jets when fully laden.

Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves have put airport expansion at the heart of Labour’s bid to kickstart the economy, arguing that the pursuit of growth “trumps” net zero commitments.

Labour is seeking to speed up legal processes in a bid to secure planning permission for a third runway before the next general election.

Mr Doyle said BA accepts that Heathrow is full but questioned whether the airport’s own model is unnecessarily complex.

He said: “We need to make sure we’re competitive and build this runway as quickly and efficiently as we can, and any opportunity to achieve that goal should be explored.

“If you can avoid moving the M25, you should avoid moving the M25. You can’t argue against the logic of that. And I think we should look at ways of potentially building a shorter runway.”

‘Serious calculations’

Thomas Woldbye, Heathrow’s chief executive, hit back at the claims, dismissing Mr Doyle’s suggestion that building over the motorway may be unnecessary.

He said: “I agree with him – if we could avoid it, we should – but it is not avoidable. It’s expensive but it’s a well-controlled, known technology solution.

“If you build a shorter runway, you have to move more motorways, like the M4 spur, which is more complicated. It’s in several layers with a railway underneath and you have nowhere to put your motorways or your building site and soil.”

He said Mr Arora’s plan would require demolition of more than 2,000 homes, compared with only about 750 for Heathrow’s own proposals, and that the difference had not been costed.

Mr Woldbye said that could make a shorter runway more expensive and that “serious calculations” indicated that only a full-length runway would keep longer-term costs to a minimum.

He said: “You’d end up with much less capacity. That does not make sense. If you want lower fares, you need the maximum number of passengers to spread costs.”

The chief executive said that Heathrow would nevertheless look at any decision from Labour recommending a cheaper alternative to its blueprint.

Asked if the airport could guarantee that costs for expansion would be capped at £49bn, Mr Woldbye said that “naturally I cannot guarantee that” since the runway would not be ready for a decade and a contingency of 20-30pc “is normal”.

He added: “We have no reason to be more expensive than we should be. We are a regulated entity, our interest is to be as efficient as we possibly can.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”

data.title: Renters’ Rights Act: What the new law means for renters and landlords
data.description:
The Renters’ Rights Bill has become the Renters’ Rights Act, after it received Royal Assent yesterday.

The Government must next set out its plans to implement the changes and bring the law into force.

Tenants who rent in the private sector will receive greater protections from eviction, unfair rent increases and greater stability of tenure.

The controversial body of legislation is the biggest change to renting in over 30 years, according to industry body The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA).

Here, Telegraph Money explains the measures laid out in the bill, what that means for landlords and when it is likely to become law.

  • Renters’ Rights Act key points
  • What was the Renters’ Rights Bill?
  • New rules in the Renters’ Rights Act
  • How will it affect landlords?
  • Will it apply to existing tenancies?

Renters’ Rights Act key points

  • End of Section 21 “no fault” evictions: instead, landlords wanting to regain possession of a rental property will need to use approved grounds
  • Rent increases can only happen once a year: any increases must reflect market rates
  • New property standards: under the new “Decent Homes Standard”, properties must be safe, of good value and good quality
  • End of fixed-term tenancies: new and existing tenancies must change to rolling agreements
  • New landlord ombudsman and database: will give tenants access to impartial and binding resolution for complaints against landlords. All landlords must sign up
  • New pet rules: tenants have the right to request pets, but landlords can stipulate pet insurance must be taken out to protect from any resulting damage
  • Rental payment overhaul: landlords cannot accept offers above the advertised asking price for rentals, and no more than one month’s rent can be paid in advance.

What was the Renters’ Rights Bill?

The Renters’ Rights Bill built on the previous Renters’ Reform Bill, introduced by the Conservatives. Now the Renters’ Rights Act, it will introduce new protections and rights for the estimated 11 million private tenants in England by reforming the current system of renting.

An end to fixed-term tenancies, longer notice periods to give renters more time to find a new home and restrictions on rent increases will give tenants more rights and landlords less control over how they manage their property and buy-to-let business.

Labour says it will “transform the experience of private renting” which will largely be achieved by ending Section 21 evictions dubbed “no-fault evictions”.

The NRLA has argued the rules could force landlords out of the sector – reinforced by a survey earlier this year by Pegasus Insight, which revealed 37pc of landlords planned to sell a property in the next 12 months while just 6pc said they intended to buy.

New rules in the Renters’ Rights Act

Evictions and serving notice:

  • Section 21 no-fault evictions will be abolished.
  • Fixed-term tenancies will be replaced with periodic tenancies, also known as rolling contracts that don’t have an end date.
  • Tenants will have a 12-month protected period at the start of their tenancy preventing landlords from forcing evictions to move back in or sell.
  • Tenants can serve two months’ notice to quit the property at any time.
  • Landlords must give four months’ notice if they wish to move back in or sell a rental property.
  • Landlords will not be able to evict a tenant in arrears until three months’ payment have been missed, up from two months while the notice period has been increased from two to four weeks.

Prices and payment:

  • Rent increases are limited to once a year and only to the market rate, which can be challenged by tenants at the first-tier tribunal if they believe it is excessive. Increases cannot be backdated.
  • Large amounts of rent cannot be demanded in advance of a tenancy starting.
  • Landlords must register with a digital database, failing to do so means that they cannot use certain grounds of possession.
  • Asking rents must be published to prevent bidding wars, making it illegal for landlords to accept offers higher than the rent advertised.

Standards and safety:

  • The Decent Homes Standard and Awaab’s Law, currently applicable to homes in the social housing sector, will be extended to private sector landlords to ensure homes are made safe, are of good value and good quality.
  • A private rented sector landlord ombudsman will be set up to provide an impartial and binding resolution for tenant complaints against landlords.

Discrimination:

  • Strengthened rights for tenants to have pets, although landlords can insist on pet insurance to cover any damage to their property.
  • End discrimination against tenants on benefits or with children.

Compliance:

  • Councils will have the power to issue civil penalties of up to £7,000 to landlords who don’t comply with the reforms, rising to £40,000 for a serious breach or repeat non-compliance with the law.
  • More strength will be given to rent repayment orders that can be issued by councils when landlords breach the law.

How will it affect tenants?

The Renters’ Rights Act will give tenants greater protections and stronger rights when they’re renting.

As outlined in the rules above, among the changes the new law will usher in, tenants must be given longer notice periods when their landlords want to move back in or sell the property they’re renting, the property must legally reach the Decent Homes Standard, and they will no longer be subject to frequent rent increases.

For any tenants with a complaint about their landlord, the introduction of an independent ombudsman will help readdress the natural power dynamic and help tenants to reach a resolution.

How will it affect landlords?

Sylvie Harris, of property consultancy INHOUS, said: “Through the abolition of Section 21 no-fault evictions, landlords will only be able to end tenancies using specific grounds under Section 8, such as rent arrears or anti-social behaviour or if they intend to sell or move into the property.”

For the latter, an extended four-month notice period must be served.

But the pressure this will place on a court system, which Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister, acknowledged during a parliamentary debate was “on its knees”, is raising concerns and has led to an amendment being tabled to review the impact of this plan on the legal system.

When the Conservative government brought forward its draft renters rights legislation, then-minister Michael Gove said that the right to make no-fault evictions would remain in place until a full review of the courts had been completed.

Currently, the average time between a landlord submitting a claim for an eviction and regaining possession of their property is more than seven months. In response to the Government’s claims that the courts will be ready for the impact of the changes, Ben Beadle, chief executive of the NRLA, said: “Seven months is an eternity for responsible landlords who may be dealing with serious rent arrears and for neighbours having to endure anti-social behaviour.”

Not only could it take longer to evict a tenant, landlords will have to endure greater levels of arrears before they apply for possession as the minimum level of arrears for eviction is bumped up from two to three months.

Landlords operating in the student let sector could also face challenges in operating their businesses efficiently if the legislation is implemented in its current form.

Ending fixed-term tenancies removes the certainty for these landlords that their property will be available to let to a new batch of students when the academic year begins.

Although grounds for possession have been included, which allows landlords of houses in multiple occupation (HMOs) to evict last year’s students to make way for a new group, one and two-bedroom student flats were not mentioned.

Banning the receipt of large sums of rent upfront, meanwhile, could put landlords off renting to international students or tenants who have had credit difficulties in the past or do not pass affordability assessments. Without the security of payment in advance, these groups may struggle to be accepted.

When will the bill become law?

The Government has announced May 1 2026 as the date when Section 21 evictions will be outlawed in England. Also from this date, fixed-term tenancy contracts must be replaced by ‘rolling’ contracts, there’ll be an end to bidding wars and landlords must include clearer rules on pets.

Will it apply to existing tenancies?

Yes, it will. We are still waiting to find out the finer details, but the plan is to make the changes to tenancies in one go rather than create a two-tier system that applies the rules to new and existing tenancies at different times.

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”

data.title: Robert Philip, Scots sports journalist who upset Canadians but movingly evoked heroism on the pitch
data.description:
Robert Philip, who has died of cancer aged 75, was a witty and pugnacious sports writer for The Daily Telegraph; his combative approach won him enemies as well as admirers, and he briefly became a national hate figure in Canada.

As the Telegraph’s senior sports columnist for two decades, Philip perfected a colourful style. Whether he was contrasting two snooker players – “Alex Higgins would rip though his matches as if he had a heavy date with Bo Derek; [Terry] Griffiths [studied] each and every shot like a Swiss craftsman crouched over a jewelled timepiece” – or holding forth on the state of Scottish football – “Being asked to become Scotland manager is clearly akin to being offered VIP front-row tickets for a Victoria Beckham concert; it’s a terrific honour, of course, but no one in their right mind would actually accept” – his judgments were memorably expressed.

He often went against prevailing opinion. When his fellow pundits lauded Paula Radcliffe as a thwarted heroine after she sank tearfully to the ground during the marathon at the Athens Olympics, Philip demurred: “Radcliffe stopped running and started blubbing for the simple reason that she had just seen gold, silver and bronze medals disappear into the distance.”

In 2001, covering the World Athletics Championships in Edmonton, Alberta, Philip made hay in his reports with the “visually unappealing” nature of “Deadmonton” and the local media’s coverage of the events (“Gritty Canadian Thrills Crowd with Gutsy Run for 42nd Spot”, read one newspaper headline). His remarks led the news bulletins in Alberta, and, as he recalled, “‘BRIT SNIT!’ (and I trust that was not a misprint) [was] the banner front page headline in The Edmonton Sun.”

Philip accepted the offer of a helicopter ride with the mayor of Edmonton to show off the city, but caused further outrage when he asserted that its top attraction was the airport departure lounge, receiving hate mail and death threats. The Globe and Mail interviewed “this snarky, heavy-set, dishevelled, unshaven, chain-smoking Scot”, its reporter making the unwelcome discovery that he was “charming and self-deprecating”.

There was far more to Philip’s writing than knockabout comedy, however, and he could write movingly about genuine sporting heroism. “Robert Philip has succeeded in reducing this grown man to tears on more than one occasion,” was the view of one Telegraph reader, speaking for many, when Philip reported from the Paralympic Games in 2000.

Robert James Philip was born in Glasgow on May 27 1950, the son of James Philip, a wayleave officer, and his wife Jean, a garage clerk. After the High School of Glasgow, he began his career as an office boy at The Sunday Post.

In 1980 he moved to London to work for Reuters, initially as football correspondent and later as tennis editor. He was determined to inject some colour into the agency’s impersonal reports, and raised eyebrows by declaring that the transfer of Charlie Nicholas from Celtic to Arsenal was “the most heralded arrival on these shores since Julius Caesar” and that Martina Navratilova was “a legend in her own mind”.

It was said to have been Philip’s fault that Reuters withdrew permission for correspondents to claim minibar bills on expenses. Nevertheless, he was acclaimed for his reporting from the Heysel Stadium disaster in 1985.

In 1988 he found a happier berth at The Daily Telegraph. He reported on every kind of major sporting fixture, including chess championships at the Kremlin, and the subjects of his incisive interviews ranged from Mike Tyson to Anna Kournikova.

He ghost-wrote the memoirs of Nick Faldo and Sandy Lyle, and in 2011 published Scottish Sporting Legends.

Philip was as funny in person as in print, although his strong Glaswegian accent prevented his wit from being fully appreciated by Sassenachs. Eventually he left London with relief, to settle in Troon.

After three marriages that ended in divorce – including one to his fellow Telegraph sports writer Sue Mott – Robert Philip found enduring happiness with Yvonne Cairnduff, whom he married in 2001; they enjoyed travelling and walking on the beach at Troon together. She survives him with five children.

Robert Philip, born May 27 1950, died August 30 2025

data.title: Germany’s public broadcasters are worse than the BBC
data.description:
The Telegraph’s publication of a revealing internal BBC dossier has proved beyond doubt the corporation’s progressive biases and its inability to accurately report on divisive flashpoints like the Israel-Hamas war.

But the BBC is not the only public broadcaster that has long abandoned impartial journalism in favour of modish activism. In Germany, the situation is arguably worse than in Britain, as at least the BBC doesn’t appear to be giving out contracts to Hamas members.

On October 19 an Israeli air strike in Gaza hit two vans operated by a local Palestinian company working for ZDF, one of Germany’s two tax-funded public broadcasters. Two people were killed, one of whom was Ahmed Abu Mutair, who ZDF described as a journalist.

The broadcaster responded with outrage. Bettina Schausten, ZDF’s editor-in-chief, expressed condolences and insisted that it was unacceptable that journalists could be attacked while doing their jobs. ZDF correspondent Thomas Reichart described those killed as “our Palestinian colleagues”.

Yet after Israel presented evidence of Mutair’s membership of Hamas’s military wing, the broadcaster abruptly ended its partnership with the Palestinian company and reclassified Mutair as a technician, not a journalist – a sleight of hand that did little to calm the storm or stop accusations that ZDF was squandering taxpayer money on terrorism.

Media organisations have been in a difficult position in reporting from Gaza, as Israel mostly sealed off the enclave to journalists in October 2023 leaving them to rely almost entirely on local producers who operate under Hamas supervision.

These are not neutral partners and will deliver material with an anti-Israeli slant. This is perhaps understandable and to some extent unavoidable, but in order to use any of the work, journalists in Western newsrooms must be able to discern this and act accordingly.

For ZDF and others, this does not appear to be happening, presumably because the anti-Israeli narrative is one that many of their reporters and editors share.

When the Allies rebuilt German media after the Second World War, they originally tried to solve the problem of bias in public broadcasting by having two organisations: ARD, the federal network of regional stations, was meant to lean slightly conservative; while ZDF, founded in 1963, was to be its liberal counterpart.

It worked, for a while. Funded by a compulsory licence fee of €18.36 per household (unlike in Britain, you pay this even if you do not watch television), the system rewards consensus and punishes dissent. Its boards are filled with political appointees, union officials and church representatives, a corporatist mosaic that produces moral conformity.

In practice, both ARD and ZDF now speak with one voice: a genteel, sermonising progressivism that equates compassion with virtue and doubt with cruelty.

The Gaza conflict has merely revealed what was already true. ARD’s flagship news, Tagesschau, delivers nightly moral instruction rather than information: lamentations for Palestinian suffering, abstract hand wringing about disproportionate responses, and an almost theological aversion to acknowledging Hamas’s atrocities. It is less a news bulletin than a televised confessional.

What makes this crisis so corrosive is its wider cultural consequence. Public broadcasters once shaped a common civic vocabulary; now they manufacture a moral mood.

They have replaced the pursuit of truth with the performance of virtue. The viewer is no longer invited to understand the world but to feel ashamed of it. In this climate, the line between journalism and activism dissolves, and the audience – compelled by law to fund it all – is reduced to a congregation before the state’s pulpit. Germany’s public broadcasters, like the BBC, no longer inform the public; they instruct it in the correct emotional posture.

The original idea of public broadcasting was meant to be a shared civic good. Now it resembles a state-funded conscience industry, shielded from scrutiny by the myth of independence. To refuse the licence fee is a criminal offence, but to question the ideology it sustains is treated as barbarism.

data.title: How the Bank of England can secure its place at the heart of the next monetary revolution
data.description:
This morning, the Bank of England will set out its last consultation on a regime for the regulation of systemic stablecoins. How the UK pursues the regulation of digital money will determine whether London joins the US in leading the next era of financial infrastructure – or watches others set the pace. 

To its credit, the Bank’s position on the regulation of systemic stablecoins has evolved. Perhaps most remarkably, the Governor has explicitly acknowledged that the holding of money can be separated from the provision of credit – a profound shift in how we think about the structure of the current financial system. 

With the 180 day UK-US Taskforce for Markets of the Future, the UK has a rare opportunity to shape how innovation in money can preserve financial centre competitiveness.

The Governor has said that stablecoins aspiring to serve as money must be backed by “risk-free” assets. That principle is sound, but not one applied to traditional bank deposits. The Governor himself recognises that commercial bank deposits – with fractional backing – are not risk-free. 

In fact, stablecoins backed one-for-one by central bank cash reserves and other (credit) risk-free assets are less risky than uninsured deposits – and their regulatory treatment should reflect this.

The Bank’s willingness to allow a portion of reserves to be held in high quality liquid assets (HQLA) like gilts, rather than exclusively in central bank, deposits is welcome progress. A sensible approach would see a significant majority – perhaps over 80 per cent – in HQLA.

This has three advantages. First, it creates a viable pathway for issuers to transition between the FCA (non-systemic) and Bank of England (systemic) stablecoin regimes, without the need to fundamentally change business models. Second, it introduces new demand for short-dated gilts, supporting the UK Government’s funding base and lowering borrowing costs. Third, it aligns the UK regime with that of other major jurisdictions, making the country a more attractive destination for stablecoin innovation.

Concerns have been raised that in a crisis a run on a stablecoin issuer could trigger a fire-sale of gilts. If that’s the case, the Governor’s suggestion that the Bank could offer Emergency Liquidity Assistance (ELA) is one way to address this. 

The Bank also looks set to propose caps on stablecoin holdings, supposedly to guard against a “precipitous” drain of deposits from banks into digital money. 

Other jurisdictions offer a useful test case. In the United States, the dollar denominated stablecoin USDC has operated for nearly six years without evidence of a destabilising flight from retail deposits. In fact, in the 70 months of data from USDC, we observe that both retail deposits and USDC’s market cap have increased during that time. 

Empirical evidence suggests neither caps, nor a ban on stablecoins paying interest are justified. By proposing both, which no other jurisdiction has deemed necessary, the UK risks putting the brakes on innovation. 

The focus should be on facilitating modernisation, not privileging legacy systems. The Bank’s proposals sit awkwardly with both the Government’s competitiveness strategy and firms’ obligations under the Consumer Duty. 

Furthermore, it is unclear how caps on holdings would be implemented once a stablecoin transitions from the FCA’s regime for non-systemic stablecoins to the Bank of England’s regime once it becomes systemic - would there be forced liquidations? Second, the caps proposed are so low that it would make stablecoins unusable for wholesale settlement in tokenised markets. 

The rationale for caps is therefore weak, and industry needs clarity on when – and on what basis – those caps would be lifted. The Governor has said it is important to “think through the implications of change before going ahead”. But there is also risk in excessive caution. We should not want the UK to deliver a regulatory framework for stablecoins that deters innovation, at the same time as other markets move ahead.

The US has legislated to provide regulatory clarity on stablecoins, and the market has responded with around 80 different announcements of companies building in this space. 

The Bank’s evolving approach to stablecoins includes some strong and welcome progress. However, more can be done. In short, with prudence and ambition, a competitive, well-regulated stablecoin regime can strengthen financial stability and secure London’s place at the heart of the next monetary revolution. 

Faryar Shirzad is Chief Policy Officer at Coinbase
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: Mansion tax fears prompt slump in London house prices
data.description:
Fears of a mansion tax have dragged down house prices at the top of the London market at the fastest rate in more than four years.

The average price of so-called “prime” houses in the capital has slumped by 4pc in the year to October, according to the latest figures from estate agency Knight Frank, marking their steepest drop since February 2021.

Tom Bill, head of UK residential research at Knight Frank, blamed “prolonged tax speculation” for the fall.

“It’s a reminder of how property taxes often come with unintended consequences,” he said. “If you tax so-called mansions, you will end up with fewer of them.”

Homeowners are bracing for a tax on expensive homes in the Budget. Rachel Reeves is considering a mansion tax that would impose a 1pc levy on the portion of a property’s value surpassing £2m in the upcoming Budget. It would mean owners of a £3m home would face a tax bill of £10,000.

Knight Frank has estimated that just over 150,000 properties in England and Wales would be hit by the proposals.

Another option being looked at is a doubling of the two highest bands of council tax.

Mr Bill said: “Whether the price slide in prime London markets continues into next year and the number of ‘mansions’ shrinks further depends on whether the Government chooses to repeat history or learn from it.”

He noted that average mansion values in prime central London have dropped by 8pc since 2012, which was the year the Liberal Democrats first proposed a tax on properties over £2m.

“Ironically, it was the original mansion tax plan that helped deflate the market,” said Mr Bill.

“It was never introduced but prompted Tory chancellor George Osborne to increase stamp duty for high-value properties in December 2014, and demand in prime markets has never fully recovered.”

Protracted uncertainty around the Budget also caused a 10pc spike in demand to rent a high-end property, rather than buy.

Average rents in prime central London rose 1.9pc in the year to October, the biggest increase since August 2024. In prime outer London, rents rose by 2pc.

David Mumby, head of prime central London lettings at Knight Frank, said: “For tenants, the gloomier things feel, the more they prioritise liquidity and cash in the bank and that is supporting strong demand in the lettings market.”

The Treasury was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Remortgage vs loan vs savings: the best ways to fund home improvements
data.description:
In a stagnant housing market, there’s a case to be made for staying put and improving the home you have.

There are plenty of clever ways to add more space, such as loft extensions and side returns – or even reconfigure the house if you don’t mind knocking a few walls down.

Home improvements and extensions will often increase a property’s value when the time comes to sell, but it can be hard to know the best way to pay for them.

Remortgaging, personal loans and paying from savings are popular options – but they all come with pros and cons that you need to be aware of before you start.

Here, Telegraph Money explains your options for financing home improvements and what you need to weigh up first:

  • Release cash by remortgaging
  • Try a second mortgage
  • Take out a personal loan
  • Pay by credit card
  • Use your savings
  • Consider equity release
  • Home improvements FAQs

Release cash by remortgaging

Anyone approaching the end of a fixed-term mortgage deal might remortgage to release equity in their home and increase their borrowing to cover the cost of home improvements.

It’s a popular move: 47pc of people who remortgaged in March increased their loan size by an average of £20,224, according to the HomeOwners Alliance. However, you should think carefully before taking this step and enlist the help of a mortgage broker to make sure you get the best possible mortgage deal.

“Going down this route may mean you can borrow larger amounts, with the rate of interest typically lower than on a personal loan,” said Mark Harris, chief executive of mortgage broker SPF Private Clients. “The monthly payments should also be lower, as you may be able to repay them over a longer term.

“However, bear in mind that the overall cost will be higher if you are making those payments over a mortgage term of 25 years, 30 years or even 45 years. There are also upfront fees and charges to consider.”

If you are planning home improvements to upgrade the energy efficiency of your property, you might be able to access more tailored borrowing options.

Mr Harris highlighted Nationwide’s Green Additional Borrowing mortgage available for existing borrowers. No interest is charged for either two or five years, and you can borrow up to £20,000.

He added: “On a smaller scale, Halifax offers a cashback reward of up to £2,000 for making home improvements, covering insulation, solar panels, heat pumps and so on, which would reduce the outlay.”

Pros

  • You can potentially borrow larger amounts.
  • Interest rates are competitive compared with other types of borrowing and can be paid back over the course of the mortgage.

Cons

  • Increasing borrowing will usually mean higher repayments and paying more interest overall.
  • If you borrow enough to drop you into a higher loan-to-value range, you may not be able to access the favourable rates that come with smaller mortgages.
  • Reducing your equity means you have a greater risk of negative equity, especially if the improvements don’t add value to your home – this is when you owe more to your mortgage lender than your property is worth.

Try a second mortgage

A second mortgage might be suitable for homeowners who decide it doesn’t make sense to refinance, perhaps in cases where remortgaging before the end of a fixed term would mean paying a hefty exit penalty, or if an existing home loan is a good deal.

Second mortgages (also known as second charge mortgages) give you a lump sum, which you repay alongside your existing home loan over a fixed term. The second mortgage is usually taken out with a different lender than the original mortgage lender, and the loan is secured against your home.

These loans are growing in popularity. In the eight months to August 2025 – the latest figures available – new business volumes were 12pc higher than in the same period in 2024, according to the Finance and Leasing Association (FLA). Based on current 2025 market trends, typical second-charge mortgage rates usually fall between 5pc and 12pc, according to mortgage broker John Charcol.

Pros

  • Allows you to borrow against your home while maintaining an existing low rate on your primary mortgage.
  • Avoids the requirement of paying early repayment charges on an existing loan.

Cons

  • If you fail to make repayments, your home is at risk.
  • Again, there’s a risk of ending up in negative equity.

Take out a personal loan

If you want to borrow £50,000 or less for home improvements, you could go for a personal loan. Some banks badge a product as a “home improvement loan”, but it’s still a personal loan where you pay back in monthly instalments with interest over a set period.

These loans are often unsecured, meaning you don’t need to use your home as collateral. This makes them very popular – home improvement loans account for 9.7pc of all borrowing, according to the lender Pepper Money, which makes them the second most popular reason for taking out a loan. Pepper Money says the average loan value for home improvements was £33,795.

If this is a route you’re considering, shop around and compare offers from different banks and lenders to find the best interest rate and terms for your circumstances. You will also need to meet affordability criteria and credit checks.

According to Moneyfacts, the cheapest personal loan charges around 5.8pc for loans of £7,500 or more. Borrowing £15,000 would cost £453.96 per month for three years, with a total repayment of £16,342.56.

Pros

  • Quick and easy application, if you meet the affordability criteria.

Cons

  • Additional debt you’ll need to budget for.
  • More expensive than a secured loan.

Pay by credit card

Using a credit card with an interest-free period on spending means you can spread the cost over many months. Currently, the maximum 0pc period for so-called purchase credit cards is 25 months, according to Moneyfacts.

While it’s unlikely that you will be able to use a credit card to pay a builder, it could be useful for buying materials, fixtures and fittings, appliances and anything else required for the improvements that you can pay for by card.

You would need a generous credit limit on your card to make this option work for larger renovations. Make sure you can repay the full amount before any interest-free period offer expires, because after that, the interest rate will soar. However, once the zero-interest period ends, there’s always the option to move the debt to a 0pc balance transfer card, as long as you meet the eligibility criteria.

Paying with plastic means you benefit from consumer protection. Card companies are jointly and severally liable for goods and services above £100 but below £30,000 under Section 75 of the Consumer Credit Act.

Pros

  • Potential for interest-free borrowing.
  • Quick application.
  • Section 75 protection.

Cons

  • If you can’t pay the full amount before the interest-free period offer is over, you’ll pay high interest charges.
  • You might struggle to get a credit limit high enough to cover your costs.

Use your savings

If you have a decent amount of money in savings, then you might decide to use some or all of the money to pay for home improvements.

While this method avoids being stung with interest payments, tread carefully if raiding this pot of money would mean putting a dent in your “rainy day” cash fund that you would be better off leaving intact – you might regret it if you’re left short in an emergency.

You might decide on using a combination of savings and other methods to preserve your all-important savings buffer.

If using savings is right for you, check before withdrawing whether there are any penalties to access certain accounts, such as those with fixed terms.

Pros

  • No debt or interest costs.

Cons

  • Your savings will be depleted, and you’ll miss out on interest and compounding.

Consider equity release

If you’re over 55, you could raise money for home improvements using an equity release mortgage.

An equity release loan is repaid when the house is sold, the owners move into care or they die – unless you opt for a plan that requires monthly repayments.

The downside is that equity release products tend to have higher interest rates than standard mortgages, and there are often substantial application, valuation, and legal fees. According to Moneyfacts, the average equity release rate is 7.49pc.

The amount you owe can build up quickly if you have opted not to make monthly repayments, because interest is charged on the original loan and any accrued interest.

Just like with any other kind of borrowing, equity release providers offer different interest rates, so it’s important to shop around for the most competitive rates and fees.

Pros

  • Quick access to cash.
  • Option for no monthly repayments.

Cons

  • Interest rates are higher than those of mainstream mortgages.
  • Over an extended period, the total debt will snowball.
  • Taking out an equity release loan will impact the amount of inheritance you leave behind.

Home improvement FAQs

Increasing your property’s value shouldn’t be the primary reason for making home improvements, but it’s still worth considering the effect they could have on your house price when you do come to sell.

Some of the most valuable improvements will be extensions that add square footage to a home, such as adding living space and bedrooms, creating more usable space for other families to enjoy. A new extension or conversion could add up to 20pc to the value of your property, but the HomeOwners Alliance recommends contacting a local estate agent for their view.

It can also be worth restoring original features such as fireplaces, original floorboards, stone flooring and wooden beams, as these can be selling points for buyers.

It’s best to check with an expert before you go ahead with any plans to see whether it could harm your home’s value. There are a handful of things that could devalue your property. For example, getting rid of the bath in a bathroom or reducing the number of bedrooms, perhaps knocking two smaller ones into one larger bedroom.

If you own the home outright, you can still borrow against its value for renovations. In this case, it’s usually called an “unencumbered” mortgage rather than a remortgage and is popular among those who don’t want to deplete their personal savings.

Since you’re likely to be applying for a reasonably low loan-to-value (LTV) mortgage, you’ll have access to the most competitive rates on the market – subject to your own credit score and affordability.

data.title: My first UK holiday in years showed me what our country has lost
data.description:
I recently visited Britain – a bit in England but mainly Wales – for the first time in quite a while.

Having lived in France as The Telegraph’s France destination expert for many years I had a smashing time, thank you. The rain barely bothered me.

This was Wales. What did I expect? Sunstroke? Anyway, the principality looks good, and richly green, in the rain. And there were plenty of pubs in which to dry out. Externally, I mean.

Along the way, though, I noticed a few things which I’d either forgotten about, hadn’t noticed before or had cropped up since my last visit.

Among them were, in no particular order:

The demise of tourist information centres

Turn up in even quite big towns – Carmarthen, Swansea – ask for the tourist office and people look blank.

“It’s all online now,” a council fellow told me. “People look it up before they leave home.”

Not everyone. Not me. For a start, before you leave home, you don’t necessarily know everywhere you’re going to be visiting. And when you get there, do you really want to scroll through the infinite reams of hysterical promo material which constitute most official tourism websites?

Wouldn’t you rather talk to a real person who may address your particular interest without your having to wade through disquisitions on zip lining and activities for single mothers with school-age children?

Or, to put it another way, every town in France has a tourist office, as do many villages, and theirs is the planet’s most visited country. Could it be that this is one thing that the French have got right?

We seem to have lost all our family-run restaurants

Every French town has an abundance. Where are they in Britain? Don’t get me wrong. We ate well – but in pubs and chain restaurants.

The establishments where husband cooks and wife serves (or vice versa) cram by the score into city-centre streets in France. Although we looked, we found precisely none in our 11 days in the UK.

The health-and-safety obsession has gone into overdrive

“Welcome. Table for two? Any allergies?” This rang out upon arrival at nearly every restaurant we visited. It may be linked to a sense that Britain spends much of its time protecting everyone against, well, everything.

As if the average Briton were rather daft. (By a breakfast room toaster: “Warning: may get hot”; at traffic lights: “Stop at red light”.)

This assumption that Britons lack both gumption and resilience seemed new to me. Then again, given the epidemic of mobility scooters on city streets – I saw more in a week in the UK than in a decade in France – the question perhaps requires further research.

Nobody stays out late

Spending happy moments in a good restaurant which, last time I looked around, was humming with diners. Now it’s 8.45pm, I look around again, and everyone’s gone. Our noisy little party is all that’s left.

Waiters and waitresses are smiling but clearly hanging about, waiting for us to leave. This happened often. Was nightlife always this way? Have all those people really gone home? At this hour?

How do Spanish visitors cope with having to eat dinner before they’ve finished lunch?

Public transport is actually pretty good

“We’re going to travel by train and bus,” I said. “Ha! Good luck with that,” replied a wiseacre British friend. But over 11 days, public transport worked terrifically – frequent, reliable, operated by drivers, especially in Wales, who had clearly taken vows of helpfulness. It got us into small towns and villages which, were they in France, wouldn’t have seen a bus unless that bus were lost.

It meant we got to appreciate the landscape without having to navigate or negotiate a way through, and faced no parking problems. Plus public transport favours encounters.

Such as the three well-dressed young women who polished off two bottles of Prosecco on the mid-morning train from Manchester Piccadilly to Chester. Thus did they tee themselves up nicely for a day at the races. “Talkative” didn’t do them justice.

Slovenly speech has become an epidemic

I do wish that the language fairy might move among my fellow citizens and restore the letter “T” to its proper place (so “lighting”, not “li’ing”; “little”, not “li’el”; “better”, not “beh’er”), and banish entirely “Know what I mean?”, “No worries”, “Enjoy”, and “Mate”. She might also rid us of split infinitives and misuse of words such as “hopefully” and “decimate”.

I bumped into all these in spoken or written English during the stay – though, oddly, less so in Wales. It is, perhaps, more difficult to speak badly with a Welsh lilt. Most of all, I’d like the fairy to disabuse us of the notion that speaking properly is a snobbish affectation.

It isn’t. In France, second-division football players usually express themselves as if they were deputy headmasters. Like everyone else in the country, they wish to be taken for clever, not stupid.

The pubs are a mixed bag

Wetherspoons, 4pm, Tuesday afternoon, Carmarthen – the enormous place is heaving. Heaving. I can barely hear my wife say: “Do we have to stay?” Who are the dozens drinking through the afternoon on a Tuesday?

Might this explain the mobility scooters? Wetherspoons obviously does a fantastic job, but this is not the image I have when, from a foreign field, I imagine a British pub.

Fortunately, the Plume of Feathers (“Wales’s smallest pub” – about the size of our lounge) is nearby, with its memories of Richard Burton and Ray Gravell.

Swansea’s No Sign bar – venerable outpost of wood, wine, Welsh whiskey and warmth amid the mini-Vegas of Swansea’s Wind Street – also fulfils a proper pub function. It lays on a Wednesday night quiz. We took part. I wish to say no more on the subject.

There are street signs everywhere, but not the ones you need

Amid the frenzy of signs clogging up Britain, the only ones you actually need – telling you where you are in any given town or city – are very often AWOL.

Please replace them. Some people really do want to find their way around Swansea.

The Welsh have mastered hymn-singing

Gosh, the Welsh have it nailed. Choral singing, too. Given the limp murmuring of much church singing in France, I’d forgotten just how sense-smacking a choir can be.

Listen to the Côr Godre’r Aran and, were you on the cusp of faith, this might tip you over.

But we’re still a friendly bunch

Everywhere – pubs, buses, restaurants, shops, hotels, cafés – we encountered real smiles, real conversation, real eagerness to be pleasant and helpful.

Easily sufficient to overlook the “Any allergies?” nonsense. I’d forgotten just how jolly, informally, agreeably friendly Britain could be.

data.title: Ozempic maker wins High Court ban on fake fat jab websites
data.description:
The drugmaker behind Wegovy has secured a major victory after the High Court granted it a blocking order for websites selling fake versions of its weight-loss jabs.

Novo Nordisk will be able to force internet providers to block access to four websites that the company accused of selling “fake and unauthorised forms of semaglutide”, the ingredient which helps people lose weight.

It claimed the websites were selling counterfeit versions of Ozempic, the drugmaker’s diabetes injection, which is used off-label for weight loss.

Novo Nordisk warned the counterfeit drugs “pose a risk to health” and were not regulated, adding: “The substances [the websites] offer to supply have not been evaluated by the UK MHRA [Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency] as being safe or of suitable quality.”

GPs have warned that people are “dicing with death” by using fake injections, with reports of drugs being wrapped up in kitchen roll without instructions when they are sent to customers.

Earlier this month, an investigation found copycat versions were being sold on Facebook and TikTok, with patients who took them reportedly losing vision and vomiting repeatedly. Both social media companies said the sale of weight-loss injections was prohibited under their policies, with TikTok removing searches and hashtags for the drugs after the investigation.

However, there are growing fears that more people could start hunting out dangerous fakes after a major jump in the price of one of the biggest approved obesity jabs.

In September, US drugmaker Eli Lilly increased the UK price of Mounjaro – known as the King Kong of weight-loss injections – by as much as 170pc following pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, to lower the cost for Americans.

It means that the drug now costs as much as £330 per injection pen, based on the highest dose. Each injection pen includes four doses. Previously, the highest-dose injection pen cost £122.

Drug crime specialists at the time warned that the increase was likely to lead to a jump in the number of copycat drugs being imported from Chinese labs and sold illicitly in the UK.

Novo Nordisk has not increased its prices. At an online pharmacy, patients can expect to pay between £99 and £199 for four doses.

It shipped in more supply of its weight-loss jabs to Britain following the price increase at its rival, after seeing more demand.

A spokesman for Novo Nordisk said of the blocking order: “We are pleased that the English High Court has confirmed the availability of website blocking as a remedy to tackle the problem of counterfeit and unauthorised semaglutide.

“This remedy is an innovative and powerful tool for taking effective action and forms an important part of the suite of enforcement remedies available against makers and sellers of unlawful products.”
